messages.content:
Is the following form standard?

Flora please pass the salsa?
Yes, the following form is standard: "Flora, please pass the salsa?" The addition of the comma after the name adds clarity and indicates direct address.

Is the following sentence standard?

N. Scott Momaday wrote the essay.

 Yes

 No
Yes, the following sentence is standard: "N. Scott Momaday wrote the essay."

Is the following name presented in standard form?

Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.

 Yes

 No

Yes, the following name, "Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr.," is presented in standard form. The abbreviation "Dr." for Doctor and the use of "Jr." to indicate a person's lineage are commonly recognized and accepted in standard English writing.

Is this form standard?

87 Wilson St.

 Yes

 No
Yes, the form "87 Wilson St." is considered standard. It is a conventional way of writing an abbreviated address that includes the street number and name.

Is the following standard?

I went to the food store today; I bought lots of fruit. Apples, grapes, and kiwi fruit were all on sale.

Yes, the following paragraph is presented in standard form:
"I went to the food store today; I bought lots of fruit. Apples, grapes, and kiwi fruit were all on sale." The sentences are properly capitalized and punctuated, and the overall structure follows standard English grammar conventions.

Choose the standard form.


my uncle lives at 31 Chase Street, Birmingham, Alabama.


My uncle lives at 31 Chase Street, Birmingham, Alabama.


My uncle lives at 31 Chase Street, Birmingham Alabama.


My uncle lives at 31 Chase Street Birmingham Alabama.

The standard form is: "My uncle lives at 31 Chase Street, Birmingham, Alabama." In this form, the street name and city are properly separated by commas, and the state name is correctly included after the city.

Choose the standard form.


1000 AD (or CE)


1000 a.d. (or c.e.)


1000 A.D. (or C.E.)


1000 AD. (or CE.)
The standard form is: "1000 AD (or CE)". In this form, "AD" and "CE" are capitalized, and there is a space before and after the "AD/CE" abbreviation. Also, there is no period after "AD" or "CE".

Choose the best form.


James Earl Jones certainly has a deep, strong, commanding voice?


James Earl Jones certainly has a deep strong, commanding voice.


James Earl Jones certainly has a deep, strong, commanding voice.


james Earl Jones certainly has a deep strong commanding voice.

The best form is: "James Earl Jones certainly has a deep, strong, commanding voice." In this form, the proper nouns are correctly capitalized, and there are commas separating the descriptors (deep, strong, commanding) to provide clarity and organization.

Choose the standard form.


the order of colors seen through a prism is as follows: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.


The order of colors seen through a prism is as follows: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.


The order of colors seen through a prism is as follows; red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.


The order of colors seen through a prism is as follows red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.

The standard form is: "The order of colors seen through a prism is as follows: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet." In this form, the sentence is capitalized correctly, there is a colon after "as follows," and the colors are separated by commas for clarity and organization.

Is the following sentence standard?

The very best onions are: those that are grown in the great state of Alabama.

Yes, the following sentence is standard:
"The very best onions are those that are grown in the great state of Alabama." In this form, the sentence is properly capitalized and punctuated. Instead of using a colon, a simple comma is used to introduce the clause "those that are grown in the great state of Alabama."

Is the following standard?

My gerbil loved to play, so I gave him a trombone.

Yes, the following sentence is standard:

"My gerbil loved to play, so I gave him a trombone."

The sentence is properly capitalized and punctuated. It also follows standard English grammar conventions.

Choose the most correct form.


I finished my dinner brushed my teeth and ran out the door.


I finished my dinner, brushed my teeth and ran out the door.


I finished my dinner, brushed my teeth, and ran out the door


I finished my dinner, brushed my teeth, and ran out the door.

The most correct form is: "I finished my dinner, brushed my teeth, and ran out the door." 

This form includes proper punctuation by placing commas between each action in the series (finished my dinner, brushed my teeth, and ran out the door). It also includes the use of the coordinating conjunction "and" before the last item in the series.

Is this form standard?

Marcus plays the piano and the guitar and the drums.

Yes, the form "Marcus plays the piano and the guitar and the drums" is considered standard. It follows the standard structure for listing multiple items in a series, using "and" before the final item.

Is the following standard?

My gerbil loved to play, so I gave him a trombone.


 Yes

 No
Yes, the following sentence is standard: "My gerbil loved to play, so I gave him a trombone." It is properly capitalized and punctuated, and it follows standard English grammar conventions.
